response:
D 

The question involves recorded car speeds (a quantitative data set of numerical values) and asks which graph type provides the *most* detail specifically for cars in one speed range (e.g., all values between 30–39 mph).

- A bar graph (or histogram) can bin speeds into ranges and show counts/frequencies per bin, but it summarizes only (losing the exact values inside each range).
- A line graph is suited to trends over time or continuous change, not to distributional detail within one range of a single variable.
- A scatterplot requires *two* variables (it shows relationships between paired data points); a single list of speeds does not naturally fit without fabricating a second axis.
- A stem-and-leaf plot uses the leading digit(s) as “stems” (the chosen speed-range intervals) and the trailing digits as “leaves” (the actual recorded values). This simultaneously shows the shape of the distribution *and* preserves every individual speed inside the chosen range, giving the highest resolution.

Thus only the stem-and-leaf plot supplies the finest-grained, non-summarized information about speeds inside any particular interval.
